title: Navigate Data
description: Browse product contexts, understand business objects and their relationships, and use global and advanced search methods to locate product data efficiently.

topics.title: The Windchill Environment and Contexts

content.text: Windchill is built around four key requirements that drive how organizations manage product data throughout the life cycle. Understanding these requirements - and how Windchill organizes data into contexts - is the foundation for navigating the system effectively.
Four Key Windchill Requirements

rows: Information Storage
Control, access, and share product data safely in a centralized repository
Information Evolution Control
Manage complex intellectual capital as products evolve through revisions and iterations
Access Control
Enforce policies based on domains, life cycle states, and user roles to protect sensitive data
Collaboration
Provide a common mechanism for sharing information across teams, departments, and organizations

content.text: Contexts Organize the Database

Contexts organize the Windchill database in alignment with how a company operates. Every context has three critical components: data unique to a product or project, a team of users with assigned roles, and policies and processes that govern how work is performed. Context types form a hierarchy: the Site context sits at the top level, followed by Organization contexts, and then Application contexts where day-to-day work happens.
Application Context Types

cards.back: Store and manage data specific to a particular product. Product contexts contain part structures, CAD documents, and associated metadata. Each product has its own team and policies.

Store shared data that can be used across multiple products - such as standard components, fasteners, and reference materials. Libraries prevent duplication by providing a single source for common parts.
Organize project plans, collaboration activities, and deliverables. Projects are used for managing timelines, tasks, and team coordination around specific initiatives.
Group interrelated projects together under a single umbrella. Programs provide visibility across multiple projects and help manage dependencies between them.

content.text: The distinction between Products and Libraries is fundamental: product contexts hold data unique to one product, while library contexts hold shared data (like standard screws or bolts) available to many products. This separation prevents duplication and ensures consistency across products.

content.prompt: Match each context type to its primary purpose:

pairs.right: Store and manage data specific to a single product
Store shared components used across multiple products
Organize plans, tasks, and collaboration activities
Group interrelated projects under one umbrella

keyTakeaways: Windchill addresses four key requirements: information storage, evolution control, access control, and collaboration
Contexts organize the database in alignment with company operations, each with data, a team, and policies
Application contexts include Products, Libraries, Projects, and Programs - each serving a distinct purpose
Libraries store shared cross-product data while Product contexts hold product-specific data

topics.title: Business Objects and Relationships

content.text: Everything managed in Windchill is a business object - any kind of business data that the system tracks and controls. Business objects serve three fundamental purposes: storing attributes (metadata like name, number, and state), storing content (files and documents), and managing relationships between objects.
Major Business Object Types

rows: Windchill Parts (WTParts)
Build product structures and form the BOM hierarchy
Parts and end items reference other objects and can be organized into parent-child structures representing the bill of materials
CAD Documents
Store CAD design files such as models and drawings
Form a CAD structure hierarchy that is linked to the product structure, keeping design data synchronized with part definitions
Documents
Store and manage any electronic content
Can form document structures and hold files such as specifications, user manuals, and reports

content.text: Object Relationships

Object relationships create two-way connections between database objects. An object can have multiple relationships with multiple other objects, forming a rich web of connections that defines your complete digital product. There are three types of relationships, each serving a different purpose.

content.prompt: Match each relationship type to its definition and example:

pairs.right: Connects a specific version to a specific version (e.g., the drill drawing describes the drill part)
Connects an object to the latest version of another (e.g., the user manual references the belt sander)
Connects a parent to a child in a structure (e.g., the belt sander uses the motor and handle)

content.text: Together, business objects and their relationships form a complete digital product definition. Parts define what the product is made of, CAD documents define how it looks and functions, documents provide supporting information, and relationships tie everything together into a coherent whole.

keyTakeaways: Business objects store attributes, content, and relationships - the three pillars of data management in Windchill
The three major business object types are Windchill Parts (WTParts), CAD Documents, and Documents
Three relationship types connect objects: Description (version-to-version), Reference (to latest version), and Usage (parent-to-child)
An object can have multiple relationships with multiple objects, forming a complete digital product definition

topics.title: Browsing and the Breadcrumb Trail

content.text: Windchill provides several navigation tools that make it easy to browse product data, inspect objects, and retrace your steps. The Navigator Browse tab, folders, information pages, and the breadcrumb trail work together to help you find and understand product data efficiently.
Browsing with the Navigator

The Navigator Browse tab lets you expand product contexts and access their pages - such as Details, Folders, and Team. Within a context, folders provide a logical storage structure. Select a folder to view its contents in the Folder Contents table. You can sort objects by clicking column headers and filter with the Search in table field to quickly locate specific items.
Mini Information Page vs. Information Page

rows: How to access
Hover over an object's thumbnail
Click the View Information icon next to an object
Detail level
Basic attributes and key relationships
All available information in one central location
Navigation impact
Appears as an overlay - no page change
Opens a dedicated page for the object
Best for
Quick preview of an object without leaving your current view
Comprehensive review of all object details, history, and relationships

content.text: Use the breadcrumb trail at the top of the page to quickly navigate through folder structures. Each element in the breadcrumb (e.g., Products > Belt Sander > Windchill Parts) is a clickable link that takes you directly to that page - saving time compared to re-browsing from the Navigator.
Recently Accessed Objects

Windchill tracks your navigation history. The Recently Accessed link in the upper-right corner shows objects you have recently visited, and the Recently Visited icon in the Navigator shows recent products and libraries. These shortcuts let you quickly return to items without re-browsing or re-searching.

content.prompt: Arrange these navigation steps in the correct order to view object information:
items: Expand the Navigator Browse tab to see available contexts
Select a folder to view its contents
Click the View Information icon to see full object details

keyTakeaways: The Navigator Browse tab and folders provide structured access to product data within contexts
The mini information page gives a quick preview on hover, while the full Information page shows all object details
The breadcrumb trail provides clickable navigation through folder hierarchies for faster browsing
Recently Accessed and Recently Visited shortcuts let you quickly return to previously viewed objects

topics.title: Search Methods

content.text: Browsing is effective when you know where data lives, but search is essential when you need to find objects quickly across contexts. Windchill provides two default search methods - global search and advanced search - along with wildcard characters and saved searches to make finding data efficient.
Global Search vs. Advanced Search

rows: Location
Search field in the Windchill header
Edit Search Criteria in the Navigator Search tab
Input
Object type dropdown + keyword field
Object type checkboxes + multiple criteria fields
Scope
Searches across all contexts the user has access to
Can be narrowed by specific criteria such as State, Type, or Number
Modes
Standard search (names/numbers only) and Index search (attributes and content)
Single focused search with configurable filter criteria
Best for
Quick keyword lookups when you know part of a name or number
Precise filtering when you need to narrow results by multiple attributes

content.text: Wildcards and Special Characters

cards.back: Replaces zero or more characters. For example, *belt* matches any object with "belt" anywhere in the name, such as "belt_sander" or "fan_belt_assembly".
Replaces exactly one character. For example, belt_sander_? matches "belt_sander_A" and "belt_sander_B" but not "belt_sander_12".
Period (.), dash (-), and underscore (_) are interpreted literally in search terms. All other special characters are ignored and replaced with spaces.

content.text: Use wildcards like *belt* to expand your search results when you are unsure of the exact name or number. The asterisk before and after your keyword ensures matches regardless of what characters surround it.
Saved Searches and Other Methods

Frequently used search criteria can be saved for quick re-access via the Search History and Saved Searches link. This is especially useful for advanced searches with multiple criteria. Beyond the two default methods, Windchill can also support faceted search (narrow results by object attributes) and classification search (find objects based on defined classification hierarchies), though these are not available out of the box.

content.prompt: Match each search feature to its description:

pairs.right: Quick keyword search from the Windchill header across all accessible contexts
Multi-criteria search using the Navigator with filters like State and Type
Stored search criteria for quick re-access to frequently used queries
Expanded search mode that searches attributes and file content, not just names

keyTakeaways: Global search provides quick keyword lookups from the header; advanced search enables precise multi-criteria filtering
The asterisk (*) replaces zero or more characters and the question mark (?) replaces exactly one character in search terms
Periods, dashes, and underscores are literal in searches; all other special characters are ignored
Save frequently used search criteria to avoid re-entering complex advanced search filters

topics.title: Exercise: Browse Data and Libraries

content.text: In this exercise, you will navigate to product data using the Navigator, explore folders, view object information using both the mini and full information pages, and browse a library context to see shared components.
This exercise uses the Belt Sander product context and the Fundamentals Library. Ensure you are logged in to Windchill with access to both contexts.

content.title: Browse Data and Libraries
content.objective: Navigate to product data using the Navigator, explore folders, view object information, and browse a library context.

steps.action: Click the Browse link in the Navigator and expand the Belt Sander product.
steps.detail: The Navigator Browse tab shows available contexts organized by type. Expanding a product reveals its navigation pages.

steps.action: Click the Details link to view the product information.
steps.detail: The Details page shows metadata about the product context, including its name, description, and team information.

steps.action: Pin the Navigator using the Pin Navigator icon.
steps.detail: Pinning keeps the Navigator expanded while you work, so it does not collapse when you click elsewhere.

steps.action: Click Folders in the Browse pane and select the Windchill Parts folder.
steps.detail: Folders organize product data within a context. The Windchill Parts folder contains all part objects for this product.

steps.action: Click the Name column header to sort objects alphabetically.
steps.detail: Sorting helps locate specific objects in large folders by arranging them in a predictable order.

steps.action: Type "belt" in the Search in table field.
steps.detail: This filters the table to show only objects matching the text in any column, helping you find items quickly.

steps.action: Hover over the belt_sander thumbnail to view the mini information page.
steps.detail: The mini information page shows basic attributes and relationships without navigating away from the current view.
steps.hint: Look under the "Uses" heading to see related objects.

steps.action: Close the mini information page and click the View Information icon next to the belt_sander end item.
steps.detail: The Information page is a central location for all data about this object, providing more detail than the mini information page.

steps.action: Note who last modified the file on the Information page.
steps.detail: The Information page shows complete modification history, including who made changes and when.

steps.action: Browse the listed links: Packages, Team, Tasks, Change Monitor, Discussions, Reports.
steps.detail: These links provide access to different aspects of the product data, showing how much information is associated with a single object.

steps.action: Click the Recent Libraries icon in the Navigator panel.
steps.detail: Libraries store parts shared across multiple products, such as standard fasteners and common components.

steps.action: Click View All and select the Fundamentals Library.
steps.detail: This library contains standard components available to all products in the system.

steps.action: Observe what Windchill parts are available in this library.
steps.detail: Standard components like screws are stored here rather than in individual product contexts, preventing duplication.

steps.action: Click Recently Accessed in the upper-right corner to view previously accessed objects.
steps.detail: This provides quick navigation back to recently visited items without re-browsing through the Navigator.

keyTakeaways: The Navigator Browse tab and Folders provide structured access to product data within contexts
The mini information page offers a quick preview, while the full Information page provides comprehensive object details
Libraries store shared components used across multiple products to prevent data duplication
Recently Accessed and Recently Visited shortcuts enable quick navigation to previously viewed objects

topics.title: Exercise: Search for Data

content.text: In this exercise, you will use global search with wildcards, perform an advanced search with multiple criteria, and save a search for quick future access.
This exercise builds on the previous exercise. Ensure you are still logged in to Windchill with access to the Belt Sander product context.

content.title: Search for Data
content.objective: Use global search, advanced search, and save a search for quick future access.

steps.action: Click the Quick Links menu, hover over Help, and select Windchill Help Center.
steps.detail: The Help Center provides documentation on Windchill features including search wildcards and syntax.

steps.action: Search for "wildcard" in the Help Center and review the Search Wildcards topic.
steps.detail: Understanding wildcards helps you create more effective searches and locate objects even when you do not know the exact name.

steps.action: Close the Help Center browser tab.
steps.detail: Return to the main Windchill window to perform your searches.

steps.action: Type "*belt*" in the global search field and press Enter.
steps.detail: The asterisk wildcards match any characters before and after "belt", returning all objects with "belt" anywhere in their name or number.

steps.action: Review the search results and note the variety of object types returned.
steps.detail: Global search returns results from all contexts you have access to, which may include parts, documents, and CAD files.

steps.action: Click Edit Search Criteria in the Search tab of the Navigator.
steps.detail: Advanced search provides more filtering options than global search, letting you narrow results precisely.

steps.action: Clear the My Favorite Types checkbox and select only the Document checkbox.
steps.detail: Filtering by object type narrows results to documents only, excluding parts and CAD files from the results.

steps.action: Remove the Number criteria by clicking in the Number field and clicking the red X.
steps.detail: Removing unnecessary criteria broadens the search within your selected type filter.

steps.action: Select State from the criteria list, set the value to "In Work", and click Search.
steps.detail: Filtering by life cycle state finds only documents currently being worked on, excluding released or cancelled items.

steps.action: In the results, hover over the object type icon for Belt Sander User Manual to confirm it is a Document.
steps.detail: Verifying the type ensures you found the correct object and that your search criteria filtered as expected.

steps.action: Click the View Information icon of the Belt Sander User Manual.
steps.detail: Opens the full Information page for this document, where you can review all attributes, relationships, and content.

steps.action: In the search panel of the Navigator, click Save This Search.
steps.detail: Saving a search lets you reuse the criteria without re-entering them, which is valuable for frequently used queries.

steps.action: Type "Belt Sander In Work Documents" in the Save This Search dialog and click Save.
steps.detail: Choose a descriptive name so you can easily identify the search later among your saved searches.

steps.action: Click Search History and Saved Searches to verify your saved search appears in the list.
steps.detail: The saved search is available with Edit Criteria and Delete actions, confirming it was stored successfully.

keyTakeaways: Global search with wildcards like *belt* finds objects across all accessible contexts quickly
Advanced search lets you combine object type, state, and other criteria for precise results
Saving a search preserves complex criteria for quick re-access without re-entering filters
The Windchill Help Center is a valuable resource for learning search syntax and features
